</div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/murals/2026/03/13/chicago-murals-mckinley-park-ashley-freda-agua-girl-ocean" >Friends paint McKinley Park viaduct mural to brighten up daily walks</a></h3><p></p><h5>Reporting by <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/genevieve-bookwalter" target="_blank" >Genevieve Bookwalter</a></h5><p><b>The inspiration:</b> Ashley Freda had one overarching goal when she reached out to her friend and muralist who goes by the artist handle "Ocean": Break the cycle of tagging and painting over and tagging again on the viaduct near her home in McKinley Park. "This viaduct was getting tagged and buffed within 24 hours. It would happen over and over again," Freda says.</p><p><b>The mural</b>: The duo spent two weeks brainstorming, sketching, creating concepts and collecting inspiration to create an idea for a piece that "people want to protect." The result features flowers, fish, frogs and female faces.</p><p><b>Key quote</b>: "Our neighborhood, they have a real appetite for art, engagement and connection. At times we lack so much here that people don’t even understand how they can participate in street art," Ocean says.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/murals/2026/03/13/chicago-murals-mckinley-park-ashley-freda-agua-girl-ocean" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p>Thanks for reading the Sun-Times Morning Edition!<br>Got a story you think we missed? <a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >Email us here.</a></p><hr><p><b>Written and curated by</b>: Phyllis Cha and Satchel Price<br><b>Editor</b>: Eydie Cubarrubia</p><hr><p><i>The Chicago Sun-Times is a nonprofit supported by readers like you. Become a member to make stories like these free and available to everyone. Learn more at </i><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjhkill-l-th/" target="_blank" ><i>suntimes.com/member</i></a><i>.</i></p>
entries.author: Satchel Price

entries.title: Firefighter seriously hurt battling Rogers Park fire has died
entries.summary: <p>A 32-year-old firefighter who was seriously hurt battling a fire in Rogers Mark Monday morning has died. </p><p>The firefighter, Michael Altman, was taken to Stroger Hospital with serious injuries and was pronounced dead Tuesday, according to authorities.</p><p>Fire officials told reporters outside Stroger Altman would have made two years of service for the department in May. </p><p>"We are heartbroken by yet another loss of our own," said Chicago Fire Department Commissioner Annette Nance-Holt. "Michael gave everything in service to the people of Chicago and we will never forget his courage and sacrifice."</p><p>It is a "profoundly difficult time for all of us," Nance-Holt said, while adding Stroger staffers "did everything they humanly could,'' to save him. </p><p>Altman, 32, was a fourth generation firefighter who served on Truck 47 in Edgewater, officials said. </p><p>"Today, the City of Chicago not only lost a public servant, but we lost a hero," said Mayor Brandon Johnson who was also on hand during the press briefing. "Let this be a constant reminder of how it important it is that we continue to put our arms around our first responders and continue to support them." </p><p>"Firefighter EMT Altman displayed an incredible amount of courage, and bravery and strength," Johnson said. "We are in deep sorrow as a city for this tragic loss."</p><p>A procession from Stroger Hospital to the Cook County medical examiner's office on the Near West Side is taking place as of 12:45 p.m., according to a Monroe District police lieutenant. </p><p>The firefighter was among those responding to a fire at the back of the four-story multi-unit building at <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/16/extra-alarm-blaze-erupts-at-a-rogers-park-building-possibly-injuring-firefighter" target="_blank" >1757 W. North Shore Ave. </a>that began around 11:20 a.m. Monday when he fell through the floor, Chicago Fire Department Deputy District Chief Kelly Burns told reporters at the scene Monday afternoon.</p><p>“He was there one second, and he was in the basement the next,” Burns said. “They had him out within, I’m going to say, under a minute from the basement.”</p><p>The cause of the fire remained under investigation. </p><p><i>This is a developing story, check back for details. </i><br></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: The Bulls' Rob Dillingham feeling, and playing, 'renewed' with new team
entries.summary: <p>This certainly isn’t the Rob Dillingham that arrived to the Bulls back at the Feb. 5 trade deadline. This isn’t even the same Dillingham from a week ago.</p><p>“I’m renewed,” the guard said with a proud grin.</p><p>One way to look at it. More accurately, Dillingham is less broken.</p><p>There’s no question that his year-and-a-half in Minnesota was not a great experience. Drafted No. 8 overall in the 2024 draft – three spots in front of where the Bulls grabbed Matas Buzelis – Dillingham went from hot prospect out of Kentucky to suspect, as in the Timberwolves were starting to question if he was a legit rotation player, let alone a talent that was warranted to be a lottery pick.</p><p>It was to the point in Minnesota that when he did get minutes, which were sparingly this season, any mistake was met with a quick hook and back to butt on the bench.</p><p>How Bulls coach Billy Donovan has handled Dillingham is coach him hard, let him get a few chances to fix mistakes, but if he’s not figuring it out then there has to be a cool-off period out of the game.</p><p>It seems to be resulting in something.</p><p>In his last three games, Dillingham has averaged 13 points, including a 15-point performance in the Memphis win, while averaging five assists and shooting 6-of-15 (40%) from three-point range.</p><p>“I’m just thankful for Coach, honestly,” Dillingham said after his latest showing. “Giving me that leash, it’s like I’m renewed. I’ve been able to play freely. My mental when I’ve been out on the court has been bad like for the last couple months, so I’m just thankful that I’m able to play, able to add confidence, able to help us win, and really, I’m just thankful to learn and keep growing.”</p><p>Donovan isn’t the only one helping Dillingham with that. Even after downing the Grizzlies, Dillingham was getting a quick post-game lift with teammate Tre Jones. The same Tre Jones that is listed an inch smaller than Dillingham, but carries more weight and way more finishing talent at the rim.</p><p>Jones’ angle game when it comes to attacking bigs is an artform, and Dillingham is all in on learning it.</p><p>“I had just said that to (Jones), ‘Brah, how do you finish like that?’ “ Dillingham said. “Then the crazy part about it is he’s not even finishing left-handed. He’s finishing right-handed over every big. That’s a big help. Even (against Memphis) I had some missed layups, but I know I can get in the lane. It’s the finishing part. I know I can finish but it’s not 100% there. Learning from him, keep asking him questions will help me for sure.”</p><p>And Jones’ secret?</p><p>“Tre’s like (thicker than me). I just said something about that, and he’s like, ‘Yeah, I got three kids I’ve been lifting.’ I was like, ‘So I gotta get some kids now?’ “ Dillingham said with a laugh. “Nah, he’s a big help for sure.”</p><p>All good stuff that Donovan likes to hear.</p><p>“When he first (came over) quite honestly, I thought he was risk-averse,” Donovan said of Dillingham. “He was so concerned about making mistakes, so trying to free him up a little bit. The biggest thing for him and these players is when you have someone like him who is very athletic but doesn’t have great size, he’s got to figure out when he gets in the lane, when he leaves his feet too early that’s when he gets himself in trouble, but I love his spirit.</p><p>“He’s eager to learn and get better, put work in, he’s always asking questions. Since he’s been here, he’s gotten more comfortable and he’s played better, and some of that might be he’s gotten more minutes too.”</p>
entries.author: Joe Cowley

entries.title: Black Caucus Chair wants to rename City College Kennedy-King-Jackson College
entries.summary: <p>In 1969, student demonstrations at Woodrow Wilson Junior College forced a renaming to Kennedy-King College to honor Robert F. Kennedy and civil rights leader Martin Luther King Jr., both of whom had been assassinated the year before.</p><p>If the chair of the City Council’s Black Caucus has her way, a third name would be added to the college in the South Side’s Englewood community: the Rev. Jesse Jackson Sr.</p><p>Ald. Stephanie Coleman (16<sup>th</sup>), whose South Side ward includes Kennedy-King, wants the City Colleges board to rename the school located at 6301 S. Halsted as "Kennedy-King-Jackson College.”</p><p>At Wednesday’s City Council meeting, Coleman plans to introduce a resolution that “formally requests” the City Colleges board to “take all necessary administrative and legal actions to effectuate” the name change.</p><p>Her resolution argues that adding Jackson’s name to the South Side college would create a “historic Civil Rights Trinity, uniting the three most influential figures of that era” on a single marquee. </p><p>She called Kennedy, King and Jackson “the visionary, the orator and the organizer” and said the name change would bring the three civil rights icons “into a single beacon of education excellence.”</p><p>Jackson spent a lifetime fighting for voting rights, economic, social and educational justice while also making two historic runs for President and freeing hostages around the world. He died last month at the age of 84.</p><p>“For over 60 years, Rev. Jackson was a tireless advocate for the Englewood community and a frequent presence at Kennedy-King College, championing the `I Am Somebody’ spirit and fighting for the educational resources and economic empowerment of its students,” the resolution states.</p><p>The resolution goes on to state that the renaming would "ensure that future generations of Chicagoans understand that the movement for equality did not end in 1968, but was sustained by the grit and grace of a Chicagoan who dedicated 60 years to the `Education of the Soul.’ “</p><p>The City Colleges website notes that Kennedy-King was “established under public pressure during the Great Depression after the city and school board, faced with a budget crisis, were forced to shut down Crane Junior College.”</p><p>Wilson’s first graduating class included Gwendolyn Brooks, the Pulitzer Prize winner who was appointed Illinois’ Poet Laureate in 1968.</p><p>U.S. Rep. Jonathon Jackson (D-Il.) said hasn’t really thought about the most appropriate way to honor his father’s memory.</p><p>“It’s flattering for the consideration, but I just haven’t thought about any of this. I will probably be in a better position in about another week,” Jonathon Jackson said Tuesday.</p><p>“Today is Mom’s birthday, St. Patrick’s Day, and I’m just on my way out to vote….I have not talked to my mother about it. I just got back in literally from Washington D.C….Tonight’s a voting night. Give me a week after we get through this election and I’ll be glad to share with you any thoughts I have.”</p><p>The proposal to make Jackson’s the third name on the Kennedy-King marquee is the second in, what will almost certainly be a string of renaming proposals.</p><p>State Rep. Kam Buckner (D-Chicago) has already proposed renaming the South side stretch of the Dan Ryan Expressway between 47<sup>th</sup> and 95<sup>th</sup> Street as the Rev. Jesse L. Jackson Memorial Highway.</p><p>Buckner was a defensive standout for the University of Illinois, where Jackson played quarterback on scholarship. They belonged to the same U of I fraternity.</p><p>In a press release and on Instagram, Buckner argued that it makes sense to rename a central artery through several historic Black neighborhoods for Jackson, who spent a lifetime building bridges.</p><p>“That corridor isn’t just concrete. It’s a front porch for Black Chicago,” Buckner said. “It’s Bronzeville, Greater Grand Crossing, Chatham, Roseland — generations of families, churches, small businesses, organizers and everyday people who built this city, even when the city was not built for them.</p><p>In 2018, Jackson joined Father Michael Pfleger and other activists in shutting down the Dan Ryan Expressway to shine a glaring spotlight on crime, poverty and unemployment plaguing inner-city neighborhoods.</p><p>Then-Chicago Police Superintendent Eddie Johnson marched shoulder-to-shoulder with Jackson and Pfleger, infuriating then-Gov. Bruce Rauner, who thought a compromise had been brokered with the protesters that would have shut down only half the northbound lanes.</p><p>“This is unacceptable. We had clear parameters that allowed the protesters to be heard while respecting law and order. Instead, they chose instead to cause chaos,” Rauner said in a posting on social media that day.</p><p>Rauner demanded that then-Mayor Rahm Emanuel “do his job” and “put an end to this kind of chaos” to keep people safe.</p><p>Emanuel refused, telling his former friend and vacation buddy on social media, “It was a peaceful protest."<br></p>
entries.author: Fran Spielman

entries.title: Madeleine Grynsztejn to leave the MCA Chicago at end of 2026
entries.summary: <p>The Museum of Contemporary Art has put on blockbuster shows featuring the work of Nick Cave, David Bowie and, most recently, Yoko Ono — and seen its endowment triple in the past two decades.</p><p>But the downtown institution has also struggled with labor issues, ones that have afflicted countless other arts organizations in the post-COVID world.</p><p>Madeleine Grynsztejn, the woman who steered the MCA during those sometimes-turbulent times, says 2026 will be her last year as the institution’s Pritzker director.</p><p>“I have enormous gratitude for what my team and I, and especially our board, has accomplished in the last 18 years. We set out to make a great museum of contemporary art, and now it is recognized globally as well as locally,” Grynsztejn said in an interview Monday.</p><p>Grynsztejn, 64, said she plans to be “fully dedicated” to the MCA through the end of 2026, before stepping down.</p><p>“Madeleine is one of the defining leaders of her generation. Her exceptional tenure has elevated the MCA Chicago to new heights — nationally and internationally — while remaining deeply rooted in and responsive to our Chicago culture and community,” Board Chair Bill Silverstein said in a statement.</p><p>The MCA this week touted the high-profile artist retrospectives held at the museum during Grynsztejn’s tenure, including those dedicated to renowned painter Kerry James Marshall, whose work explores the African-American experience and Takashi Murakami, the Japanese artist, sculptor and filmmaker.</p><p>Grynsztejn also highlighted the museum’s <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/art/2025/08/21/bilingual-museums-spanish-chicago-dual-language-programs-art-mca" target="_blank" ><u>bilingual initiative</u></a>, which means visitors can now find Spanish descriptions in the galleries, as well as bilingual programming for families and kids.</p><p>Grynsztejn was also clearly delighted with the public reception for the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/art/2025/10/17/yoko-ono-art-exhibition-museum-contemporary-art-chicago-mca" target="_blank" ><u>Yoko Ono retrospective</u></a>, which closed last month.</p><p>After opening at London’s Tate Modern and stopping in Germany, Chicago was the show’s only U.S. stop. The exhibit featured more than 200 items that showcased the breadth of Ono’s prolific artistic output and activism over seven decades.</p><p>“It was an extraordinarily warmly received and popular as well as accessible exhibition,” she said, noting that the show had the highest attendance of any show since 2019.</p><p>But the museum has also struggled with staffing issues. In 2024, staff there <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/money/2024/02/14/museum-contemporary-art-chicago-workers-form-union-afscme" target="_blank" ><u>formed a union</u></a>, citing, among other things, “burnout.”</p><p>A letter from MCA employees cited being overworked “through near-constant exhibition turnovers, hosting in-person programming on par with a pre-pandemic calendar, tight publication deadlines.” Because of staff shortages, employees said they work overtime and beyond their job descriptions. “In short, we are doing more (exhibitions, programs, publications) with less (staffing and funding),” they added.</p><p>The move to unionize followed the formation of a union at the Museum of Science and Industry in 2023, as well as at other high-profile Chicago cultural institutions.</p><p>“The MCA was not alone during a period in COVID when among other arts organizations we were closed for eight months, and for an organization whose number one job is to have its doors open every day for art to meet people, that was a period of unbelievable challenge …,” Grynsztejn said. “I am immensely proud of the way that our team came together in the face of these challenges, which were not uncommon across the board in museums in Chicago and beyond.”</p><p>Grynsztejn wouldn’t say specifically what she has in mind for the next phase of her life, but she said she intends to remain in the city that has been her home off and on for 30-plus years.</p><p>“I see working more directly with artists at scale in my future,” she said.<br></p>
entries.author: Stefano Esposito

entries.title: Man accused of shooting mother and CPD sergeant in Pullman
entries.summary: <p>A Chicago man was charged with allegedly shooting his mother and a Chicago police sergeant Saturday night in Pullman on the Far South Side. </p><p>Treavor Taylor, of the 11300 block of South Indiana Avenue, was charged with two counts of attempted first degree murder, aggravated battery of a peace officer and aggravated battery by discharging a firearm among other charges, police said. </p><p>The sergeant — driving in a marked police car — was in the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/15/chicago-police-sergeant-on-duty-shot-pullman" target="_blank" >11200 block of South Corliss Avenue</a> around 11 p.m. Saturday when she were shot in the leg, according to police. That location is about a block south of the Calumet District CPD station.</p><p>The sergeant drove to a nearby hospital, where she was reported in good condition. </p><p>Minutes earlier, Taylor allegedly shot his 63-year-old mother inside a residence in the 11300 block of South Indiana Avenue, according to police sources.</p><p> Taylor, 26, is scheduled to appear in court Tuesday. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Chicago Housing Authority appoints Keith Pettigrew as new CEO
entries.summary: <p>The Chicago Housing Authority appointed a new CEO: Keith Pettigrew, the executive director of the District of Columbia Housing Authority.</p><p>The vote comes after 18 months without a permanent CEO at the housing authority and marks a win for the CHA board over Mayor Brandon Johnson. The CHA voted seven to two with one abstention at its Tuesday board meeting.</p><p>The Sun-Times previously reported that Pettigrew said he withdrew his application for the CHA job in May 2025.</p><p><i>This is a developing story.</i> </p>
entries.author: Lizzie Kane

entries.title: Judge comes out swinging on whether trans patrons must be admitted to a female-only spa
entries.summary: <p>Context is important.</p><p>For instance, before we dive into today's topic, I would ask you to consider the respectable business establishment, Dick's Sporting Goods, which I patronize and recommend for its wide selection and affordable prices.</p><p>Now, imagine a roomful of detectives, hunting for clues. We could refer to them colloquially as "dicks."</p><p>Have I softened you up for the opening line of 9th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals Judge Lawrence VanDyke's dissent in <a class="Link" href="https://reason.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/2026-03-12-Olympus-Spa.pdf" target="_blank" >“Olympus Spa v. Andretti,” </a>which dropped last week, setting the legal world abuzz? Let's see.</p><p>"This is a case about swinging dicks," VanDyke began. "The Christian owners of Olympus Spa — a traditional, Korean, women-only, nude spa — understandably don't want them in their spa. Their female employees and female clients don't want them in their spa either. But Washington State insists on them. And now so does the 9th Circuit."</p><p>The case is relevant in our world where a basic fact of nature — some people born male want to live as women, and vice versa — has become a political hobbyhorse that will only see more use as the mid-term elections approach and the Republicans seek to light a fire under their base, chilled by the skein of jaw-dropping disasters their policies have wrought.</p><p>Back to VanDyke, who recognizes the shock value of his language. He continues:</p><p>"You may think that swinging dicks shouldn’t appear in a judicial opinion. You’re not wrong. But as much as you might understandably be shocked and displeased to merely encounter that phrase in this opinion, I hope we all can agree that it is far more jarring for the unsuspecting and exposed women at Olympus Spa — some as young as thirteen — to be visually assaulted by the real thing."</p><p>Shock is a funny thing. While it seems natural, to those shocked, it also is very specific to certain times and places. White Southerners in the 1950s were shocked at the thought of Black people sharing their swimming pools. Meanwhile, in Japan, men and women bath naked together in public baths without batting an eye. It's cultural, not natural.</p><p>Conservatives, remember, are capable of amazing feats of imagination, when the spirit moves. They have no trouble confabulating a fetus the size of a grain of rice into a full grown "baby," nor any hesitation dictating the most intimate decisions of biological women based on that feat of fantasy. Considering trans women as women should be a piece of cake.</p><p>Instead, we have VanDyke deploying words like "woke" and "man" with barely concealed contempt — to me, that is the shocking part. There is no question there are slivers of life where the existence of trans people pose challenges to be sorted out. Girls high school athletics come to mind. It's that sense of angry grievance that is sad and puzzling. Why must MAGA always be the victims?</p><p>The ruling is over 100 pages long, and, not being a lawyer — though surrounded by them, I nearly am, by osmosis — I summarize it reluctantly.</p><p>But the 9th circuit seems to have ruled that Olympus Spa erred by challenging a state law not on its own merits but on federal First Amendment grounds. That its right of free speech was trodden upon by forcing them to rewrite their admission rules and its<b> </b>freedom of religion by violating their sense of Christian modesty (the modesty argument doesn't work as well for Muslim girls who want to wear a hijab to school. Why is that?</p><p>If VanDyke's word choice at the start of this column bugs you, you are not alone. The other judges of the 9th circuit — 27 of them — added an <i>en banc</i> response to VanDyke's dissent. It begins:</p><p>"The American legal system has long been regarded as a place to resolve disputes in a dignified and civil manner or, as Justice [Sandra Day] O’Connor put it, to 'disagree without being disagreeable.' It is not a place for vulgar barroom talk. Nor is it a place to suggest that fellow judges have 'collectively lost their minds,' or that they are 'woke judges' 'complicit' in a scheme to harm ordinary Americans. That language makes us sound like juveniles, not judges, and it undermines public trust in the courts. The lead dissent’s use of such coarse language and invective may make for publicity or entertainment value, but it has no place in a judicial opinion."</p><p>We certainly live in coarse times. I would suggest coarse language is the least of our concerns. It is the crudeness and vulgarity within people's hearts that is the true offense.</p>
entries.author: Neil Steinberg

entries.title: Northwestern OT Caleb Tiernan is a college rarity — and a future pro
entries.summary: <p>Offensive tackle Caleb Tiernan has played for two different head coaches, two different offensive coordinators and in two different home stadiums. He’s played in a conference with 14 teams — and one with 18 teams.</p><p></p><p>What’s unusual is that Tiernan did it all at one place.</p><p></p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/college-sports" target="_blank" >Northwestern</a> offensive tackle is the rarest of modern college athletes — one that stayed at the same school for five years. He’s leaving campus having played more than 3,000 snaps and having started the last 38 games at left tackle.</p><p></p><p>Tiernan stuck around, even as Northwestern changed coaches, the Big Ten expanded and college football turned into a free-for-all. He rode a rollercoaster — the Wildcats went 3-9 and 1-11 the two years before Pat Fitzgerald was fired and then 8-5, 4-8 and 7-5 under David Braun — and didn’t ask to get off.</p><p></p><p>“Transferring is not always bad,” Braun said Tuesday. “But this illusion that transferring is always a good thing is a bunch of B.S.”</p><p></p><p>The Livonia, Michigan, native has a simple explanation for why he stayed.</p><p></p><p>“When I made a commitment as a high schooler, I didn’t just commit to the staff,” he said. “I committed to the guys in the locker room and to the school.”</p><p></p><p>Soon, he’ll get to know a new locker room. Tiernan is expected to be picked in Round 2 or 3 of next month’s NFL draft. Scouts from 30 of 32 teams — including the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> — watched Tiernan work out at Northwestern’s on-campus pro day Tuesday. Some asked about why he never transferred.</p><p></p><p>“Really, you just hope it shows that I’m committed and I just love those guys in the locker room,” he said.</p><p></p><p>At 6-foot-8, 323 pounds, Tiernan was the tallest tackle at the NFL Scouting Combine — and one of the best pass-blockers in the draft. Physically, he’s eerily similar to Ozzy Trapilo, another 6-8 player, who, the Bears took in Round 2 last year. Unlike Trapilo, though, Tiernan’s arms are considered short for his position. They’re 32 ¼ inches long — below the 33-inch ideal for the position.</p><p></p><p>Because of the arm length question, Tiernan has told scouts he’d be comfortable playing guard at the next level. That’s what happened to Peter Skoronski, Tiernan’s former Northwestern teammate. The Titans drafted him No. 11 overall in 2023 as a tackle, though there were concerns about his short arms. He quickly became of the league’s best guards, starting all but three games over the past three years.</p><p></p><p>The Bears don’t need a guard, but Tiernan’s versatility could be attractive. He could help fill in for the injured Trapilo this year and move inside in the future.</p><p></p><p>Skoronski has counseled Tiernan about the pre-draft process. Skoronski and Rashawn Slater, both tackles, are two of the Wildcats’ three first-round picks of the last 20 years. Tiernan could become the first Northwestern player taken in either Rounds 2 or 3 during that same span.</p><p></p><p>Braun is proud of that offensive line legacy, though quick to give Tiernan the credit for his own success. He stuck around.</p><p></p><p>“I think sometimes with college recruiting it’s so easy to say, ‘Look at all these guys we got drafted — it’s all because of us,’” Braun said. “No. … You’re not the only one responsible for that success. It’s about finding the right young men.”<br></p>
entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: Nick Foligno anticipating Blackhawks' future while focusing on Wild's present
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a> are confronting the challenge of rebuilding a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2026/03/13/blackhawks-leadership-change-connor-bedard-tyler-bertuzzi-nick-foligno" target="_blank" >leadership</a> structure with a lack of experienced leaders. Their youngsters must open their mouths and grow into those roles.</p><p>The Wild, meanwhile, are confronting the opposite challenge: How does leadership work when a locker room is absolutely stacked with experienced leaders?</p><p>Two guys who entered this season as captains elsewhere — former Canucks defenseman Quinn Hughes and former Hawks forward Nick Foligno — have been acquired via trade. </p><p>They've joined longtime Wild captain Jared Spurgeon, alternate captains Kirill Kaprizov and Marcus Foligno and four other veterans in their 14th-to-16th NHL seasons (Marcus Johansson, Mats Zuccarello, Vladimir Tarasenko and Jonas Brodin).</p><p>The old guard's lack of possessiveness and egoism is making it work.</p><p>"The players that have come into our team, if they have something to say, they say it," Wild coach John Hynes said Tuesday. "Our leadership structure with our players that [have been] here is open. They're receptive to information or thoughts from new guys.</p><p>"Part of being a leadership group isn't just the three guys who wear the letters. There's usually five to six to seven guys on a team, particularly on a team like ours where everyone can be a leader in their own way."</p><p>For Nick Foligno, who returned to the United Center Tuesday less than two weeks after departing it on deadline day, transitioning from captaining the Hawks to holding no official status with the Wild hasn't actually been too difficult.</p><p>The reprieve from the tough decisions and individual pressure has been nice in a way, and he still feels comfortable being himself in this new environment. </p><p>"It's a fun group to come into because they have such a veteran presence," Foligno said. "It's fun to walk into a room that knows who they are, knows what makes them great and can get there on an nightly basis."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Nick Foligno earned his first assist with the Wild on this Ryan Hartman goal last week: <a href="https://t.co/OBTE2mQNIR">pic.twitter.com/OBTE2mQNIR</a></p>— Ben Pope’s Video Clips (@BenPopeCSTclips) <a href="https://twitter.com/BenPopeCSTclips/status/2033998014524666144?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 17, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Besides, it's not like anybody expects Foligno to be quiet just because no letter is stitched onto his green jersey. The Wild acquired him largely because of his leadership and ability to inject energy into a group, which should be valuable in the playoffs.</p><p>Hynes joked that especially whenever the two Foligno brothers are together — even though they've yet to play side-by-side on the ice as Marcus nurses a lower-body injury — it is "tough to get a conversation in" edgewise.</p><p>Foligno got dinner with many of his former Hawks teammates Monday in Chicago (Connor Bedard picked up the tab), and Foligno's wife and children will be in attendance Thursday in St. Paul for the two teams' rematch.</p><p>"It's such a rush when you get traded, especially on a game day," Foligno said. "You just get out of their way. It was nice to say goodbye, in a way, and just catch up and see how things have been."</p><p>"It's special that I get to come back here this quick and...thank [Hawks] fans and the organization for believing in me. As hard as a couple parts of the seasons were, I loved my time here. I absolutely cherished being in the leadership role."</p><p>The majority of his brain is now focused on the Wild, as it should be, and the high-stakes hockey that awaits them. </p><p>They entered Tuesday having lost four of his first five games, a slump that has probably locked them into third place in the Central Division standings and set up a daunting first-round series against the Stars or Avalanche.</p><p>But there's still a part of his brain that remains very emotionally invested in the Hawks' future, too. He will be watching closely from afar.</p><p>"I'm excited to see where it goes," he said. "I leave proud and anticipating what's to come. That was hard to say goodbye to because you almost want to be here for those moments — when they get it, when they figure it out. And they're going to."</p><p></p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: White Sox fans, think you’ve had it bad? Try rooting for rotten Tottenham Hotspur.
entries.summary: <p>They took their seats — half a dozen of them — in a North Side bar Sunday morning that was otherwise a sea of Liverpool F.C. red.</p><p>Frankly, I wouldn’t have been surprised if no Tottenham Hotspur fans had showed up at A.J. Hudson’s, so awful has our English Premier League season been.</p><p>“It’s foolhardy to have too much hope. … We’ve been disappointed too many times,” grumbled one of those fans, Todd Hawes, 56, of Bartlett.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-d00000" name="image-d00000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e39a9d8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5068x2844+0+268/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F64%2F44%2F9e36faf04865997e5dc7da99515f%2Ftottenham-260319-033.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f4bc47c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5068x2844+0+268/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F64%2F44%2F9e36faf04865997e5dc7da99515f%2Ftottenham-260319-033.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e39a9d8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5068x2844+0+268/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F64%2F44%2F9e36faf04865997e5dc7da99515f%2Ftottenham-260319-033.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Tottenham fans Todd Hawes, right, and Marc Delis watch their team play Liverpool F.C. at A.J. Hudson’s Public House on Sunday. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>You might be unfamiliar with Tottenham, the soccer club, but the last two times the Chicago Bears were in London, they played at Tottenham’s $1.4 billion stadium — one of the best in Europe — and with a retractable pitch specifically designed to host NFL games.</p><p>One of the world’s wealthiest soccer clubs, Tottenham are currently standing on a precipice, facing a doomsday scenario — the once unthinkable — if they can’t stop the rot: relegation.</p><p>This is shaping up to be, for all of the wrong reasons, one of the biggest stories in sports outside of the U.S.</p><p>For the richest, most successful clubs in England’s “Top Flight,” the “r” word is what happens to other, less important teams — a quaint custom that keeps things fresh and also allows the so-called minnows to get a whiff, however brief, of one of the most glamorous leagues in sports.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-b40000" name="image-b40000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1823083/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6d%2F3f%2F42b99b5d43e6b6c1f3b88090e971%2Ftottenham-260319-011.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7869014/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6d%2F3f%2F42b99b5d43e6b6c1f3b88090e971%2Ftottenham-260319-011.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0d2f555/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6d%2F3f%2F42b99b5d43e6b6c1f3b88090e971%2Ftottenham-260319-011.jpg" alt="TOTTENHAM_260319-011.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0d2f555/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6d%2F3f%2F42b99b5d43e6b6c1f3b88090e971%2Ftottenham-260319-011.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f6ac7bc/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6d%2F3f%2F42b99b5d43e6b6c1f3b88090e971%2Ftottenham-260319-011.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0d2f555/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F6d%2F3f%2F42b99b5d43e6b6c1f3b88090e971%2Ftottenham-260319-011.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A young Liverpool fan reacts to his team scoring a goal. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>It works like this: At the end of the season, the three teams with the fewest points after playing 38 games are relegated to a lower division; three teams come up from that division to take their place.</p><p>It's not a perfect comparison, but it would be like the Chicago Cubs dropping down to the minors and having to play the Omaha Storm Chasers or the Toledo Mud Hens week in and week out.</p><p>Tottenham’s best players would most likely turn up their noses at playing in England’s second tier and demand to leave. And it’s been estimated that the team would lose about $340 million in revenue from falling out of the Premier League. On top of that, Arsenal, our hated North London rivals, appear to be on course to be crowned Premier League champions at the end of the season.</p><p>“I love it! I’m praying for it!” said Kojo Asante, 31, an Arsenal fan who lives in the Uptown neighborhood and who was at A.J. Hudson’s Sunday. “What a great year it would be if Arsenal wins it all and Tottenham goes down!”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-8d0000" name="image-8d0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/30c0c64/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5841x3278+0+309/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff3%2F87%2F33c3e35b4f4091132e6279fef3d4%2Ftottenham-260319-006.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/59f3bfb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5841x3278+0+309/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff3%2F87%2F33c3e35b4f4091132e6279fef3d4%2Ftottenham-260319-006.jpg srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0575f9a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5841x3278+0+309/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff3%2F87%2F33c3e35b4f4091132e6279fef3d4%2Ftottenham-260319-006.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/65b8c6a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5841x3278+0+309/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff3%2F87%2F33c3e35b4f4091132e6279fef3d4%2Ftottenham-260319-006.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0575f9a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5841x3278+0+309/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff3%2F87%2F33c3e35b4f4091132e6279fef3d4%2Ftottenham-260319-006.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Arsenal fan Kojo Asante, center, watches rival team Tottenham. “What a great year it would be if Arsenal wins it all, and Tottenham goes down!”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>I grew up watching Spurs, thrilling at the sight of the electric green pitch as I made my way through the turnstiles — for a mere $4 — to the terraces. English midfield maestro Glenn Hoddle played for my team in those days, along with Argentinian World Cup winners Ossie Ardiles and Ricky Villa. We won trophies, played gorgeous football.</p><p>This season, I’ve witnessed silkier skills watching my son’s third-grade soccer team, on their way to taking first place in their Sunday league. Losing star striker Harry Kane to German giants Bayern Munich was devastating; so too was saying goodbye to South Korean superstar Son Heung-Min, who now plays in the U.S. for LAFC. Injuries to key players haven't helped.</p><p>Tottenham have dithered in acquiring new players, waiting too long or not coughing up enough money to get the best ones.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-e40000" name="image-e40000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/82ae108/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7979x4478+0+422/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2d%2F0c%2Fa132ed3049fb9b205a120ca96148%2Ftottenham-260319-015.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/221a869/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7979x4478+0+422/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2d%2F0c%2Fa132ed3049fb9b205a120ca96148%2Ftottenham-260319-015.jpg data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/62785e2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7979x4478+0+422/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2d%2F0c%2Fa132ed3049fb9b205a120ca96148%2Ftottenham-260319-015.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/62785e2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7979x4478+0+422/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2d%2F0c%2Fa132ed3049fb9b205a120ca96148%2Ftottenham-260319-015.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a1cfc17/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7979x4478+0+422/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2d%2F0c%2Fa132ed3049fb9b205a120ca96148%2Ftottenham-260319-015.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/62785e2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7979x4478+0+422/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2d%2F0c%2Fa132ed3049fb9b205a120ca96148%2Ftottenham-260319-015.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Tottenham fans Stefano Esposito, left, and Jaesoo Lim react to their team almost scoring a goal during Sunday’s game against Liverpool F.C. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>With the fare as flat as a deflated souffle, the players are routinely booed off the pitch.</p><p>“For the love of God,” I scream at the TV, “do you really want to be the first Tottenham team to go down in almost 50 years!”</p><p>On Sunday, against the odds, something jolted this woeful bunch into action. Tottenham almost never win at Anfield, Liverpool’s stadium, and last year they thrashed us 5-1 there.</p><p>Sunday’s was a scrappy game and, predictably, Liverpool struck first, scoring a goal Tottenham’s keeper should have saved.</p><p>“Don’t talk to me!” snapped Spurs fan Jorge Guzman, 32, of Bellwood, after Liverpool went ahead.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Tottenham fans Jorge Guzman, right, and Sandra Lopez, center, cheer after their team scored a goal Sunday. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>But Liverpool aren’t the team they were last year, when they won the Premier League. Their defense is leaky, they are wasteful in front of goal.</p><p>With the introduction of two of Tottenham’s more creative players late on, Liverpool’s lead looked precarious. The equalizer came in the final minute of the game, when attacker Randal Kolo Muani latched onto a long ball from the Spurs keeper, held off a Liverpool defender, before passing to streaky striker Richarlison (yes, he has only one name), who slotted the ball into the net.</p><p>Pandemonium in the bar — well, as much pandemonium as you can have with six Spurs fans.</p><p>The game ended in a 1-1 draw, keeping us a single point above the relegation zone. But we’re not safe yet. There are eight more games to play. Anything can happen.</p><p>“I don’t think we will go down,” Guzman said. “But it will be very close.”<br></p>
entries.author: Stefano Esposito

entries.title: 75 por ciento de votantes demócratas de Chicago apoyan más a candidatos que lucharon contra ICE, encuesta
entries.summary: <p>Los votantes demócratas e independientes siguen viendo la aplicación de la ley de inmigración como un tema principal en Chicago y los suburbios cercanos, y es más probable que apoyen a un candidato que haya protestado contra el Operativo Midway Blitz del presidente Donald Trump, según una nueva encuesta encargada por una coalición progresista.</p><p>Varios candidatos en las elecciones congresionales y estatales han hecho de su postura en contra del Servicio de Inmigración y Aduanas (ICE) un punto focal de sus campañas, incluyendo a los principales candidatos demócratas al Senado. Las elecciones primarias del martes servirán como una prueba de si los mensajes de campaña sobre la lucha contra ICE fueron efectivos, y cómo han cambiado las opiniones sobre la inmigración durante el segundo mandato de Trump.</p><p>Cuando se les preguntó sobre ICE, el 63% tuvo una opinión desfavorable, mientras que el 31% tuvo una opinión favorable. Esto se desglosa de manera drástica por partidos políticos, con el 90% de los demócratas viendo a ICE de manera desfavorable en comparación con el 9% de los republicanos. Entre los independientes, la cifra fue del 62%.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-fc0000" name="module-fc0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Esto se desglosó en partidos políticos, con el 90% de los demócratas calificando a ICE como desfavorable y el 9% de los republicanos opinando lo mismo. Otro 62% de los independientes también lo calificaron como desfavorable.</p><p>En Chicago, el 75% de los encuestados en general tenían una opinión desfavorable de ICE. En el resto del Condado de Cook, el 62% de los encuestados vio a ICE de manera desfavorable, y en los condados circundantes, el 56% tuvo una opinión desfavorable del ICE.</p><p>Para los encuestados con una opinión desfavorable del ICE, el 75% estaba en Chicago, el 62% estaba en el resto del Condado de Cook y el 56% estaba en los condados circundantes.</p><p>Aproximadamente el 70% de los votantes demócratas probables que participaron en la encuesta dijeron que la posición de un candidato sobre ICE era el tema más importante o muy importante para cómo emitirían su voto. Casi dos tercios de los encuestados dijeron que estarían más propensos a apoyar a un candidato que ha estado activo en protestas contra ICE.</p><p>Cerca del 14% de los participantes dijeron que habían estado en contra de las acciones de ICE, y cerca de una cuarta parte de los demás dijeron que desearían haber podido hacer más. Aquellos que tomaron medidas eran más propensos a ser residentes jóvenes de Chicago que se consideran votantes liberales.</p><p>Esas acciones incluían firmar peticiones contra ICE (32%), contactar a funcionarios locales (27%), unirse a vigilias o protestas (24%), rastrear a ICE a nivel local (16%) y hacer sonar silbatos para alertar a sus vecinos sobre la presencia de ICE (11%).</p><p>La encuesta de 800 votantes registrados en el mercado de medios de Chicago, que incluye los condados de Cook, DeKalb, DuPage, Kane, Kankakee, Kendall, McHenry y Will, se realizó del 1 al 4 de marzo.</p><p>Las entrevistas se llevaron a cabo mediante llamadas telefónicas en vivo y respuestas de texto a la web. Según el encuestador, los demócratas autodenominados superaron en número a los republicanos en la muestra, con un 44% frente a un 22%. Otro 33% se identificó como votantes independientes.</p><p>De los encuestados, el 33% aprobó el trabajo que Trump está haciendo como presidente, mientras que el 64% lo desaprobó. Sobre la gestión de Trump en inmigración, el 35% aprobó, mientras que el 63% dijo que desaprobaba.</p><p>La encuesta fue realizada por GBAO y encargada por Hands Off Chicago, coalición de grupos progresistas de la comunidad que incluye a la Coalición por los Derechos de Inmigrantes y Refugiados de Illinois (ICIRR), Equality Illinois y la Federación de Trabajo de Chicago. El grupo también llevó a cabo cuatro grupos focales en persona, dos con progresistas y liberales blancos, uno con latinas y otro con hombres negros. También realizaron un grupo focal en línea con votantes indecisos.</p><p>La exposición a ICE jugó un papel en la reacción de los votantes hacia la agencia, y el 73% de aquellos que vieron redadas en sus vecindarios tienen una opinión desfavorable de la agencia. La cifra disminuyó para aquellos que no tuvieron redadas en su vecindario, con un 51% de esos votantes viendo a ICE desfavorablemente.</p><p>La encuesta también encontró que el 29% de los latinos dijeron que vieron agentes de ICE en sus comunidades personalmente, más del doble que la tasa de votantes negros o blancos.</p><p>Cerca del 75% de los encuestados dijeron que la inmigración es buena para el país, mientras que el 83% dijo que apoya proporcionar un camino hacia la ciudadanía para las personas en Estados Unidos sin documentación.</p><p>Al menos 2,800 personas fueron arrestadas en Illinois durante el Operativo Midway Blitz entre mediados de septiembre y mediados de octubre, principalmente en el área de Chicago, según datos de ICE obtenidos por el Proyecto de Deportación de Datos y declaraciones sobre arrestos de la Patrulla Fronteriza hechas por el Departamento de Justicia en la corte.</p><p>El 12 de septiembre, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2025/11/18/silverio-villegas-gonzalez-asesinado-por-agente-de-ice-en-area-de-chicago-tenia-cocaina-en-el-sistema-autopsia" target="_blank" >Silverio Villegas González</a>, un padre y cocinero originario de México, le dispararon fatalmente a “quemarropa” mientras supuestamente intentaba huir de una parada de tráfico. El Operativo Midway Blitz disminuyó a mediados de noviembre.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: Cubs pitcher Cade Horton appears to be back on track
entries.summary: <p>MESA, Ariz. — Those three home runs <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" >Cubs</a> pitcher Cade Horton allowed against the Rangers last week were much less concerning after Horton’s next start, a 10-strikeout performance against the Guardians on Monday night in Goodyear, where he allowed three hits and one walk.</p><p>Horton commanded his changeup exceptionally well against the four lefties in Cleveland’s lineup, generating 21 swings and misses on 74 pitches, 11 of which were changeups. He stood tall afterward, as pleased as one would expect 10 days before Opening Day.</p><p>“I felt like tonight I put it all together,” Horton said.</p><p>The swings and misses were two more than Horton’s season high in 2025, when he pitched like the Cubs’ best starter after the All-Star break and finished second in National League Rookie of the Year voting to the Braves catcher Drake Baldwin.</p><p>It would not have been surprising to see Horton named the Cubs’ Opening Day starter on March 26, but he is crafting the look of a staff ace who will get that chance very soon in his career. The Cubs are going with 35-year-old veteran left-hander Matthew Boyd, who went 14-8 with a 3.21 ERA in a team-high 31 starts and made the NL All-Star team last season.</p><p>“I’m so excited for him,” Horton said. “There’s nobody more deserving than him. He goes about his business the right way. Just the ultimate teammate. He just really does put the organization above himself. I think it’s really cool to watch him and just be able to share a clubhouse with him, so I’m really excited to watch him go out there and shove.”</p><p>Horton is lined up to pitch the Cubs' second game on March 28. He went 11-4 with a 2.67 ERA over 22 starts and one relief appearance covering a closely monitored 118-inning workload. Look for him to add innings in his second season after he finished strong with a 1.03 ERA over his last 12 starts.</p><p>A right rib fracture prevented him from pitching in a postseason that saw the Cubs defeat the Padres in a three-game wild-card series and lose to the Brewers in five games in the NLDS. Horton averaged a fraction above five innings and 78 pitches per performance during the regular season.</p><p>“My job is to go out there and get outs,” Horton said. “It doesn’t matter how many outs, just go out there and get outs and when they take the ball from me they take the ball from me. Hopefully I get more innings this year and put more on my workload. That’s something I want. We’ll see where we’re at.”</p><p></p><p></p><p></p><bsp-listcarousel class="PageListE" data-module data-column-count=3 data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" entries.title: Gobernador se burla de Bovino en medio de anuncio de supuesta jubilación de jefe de la Patrulla Fronteriza
entries.summary: <p>El gobernador JB Pritzker celebró el lunes la supuesta jubilación del comandante de la Patrulla Fronteriza, Gregory Bovino, quien lideró las agresivas —y a veces mortales— campañas de deportación del presidente Donald Trump en Chicago y otras ciudades.</p><p>“Greg Bovino no podrá simplemente irse, será responsabilizado por el daño que le ha hecho a nuestra nación”, dijo Pritzker en la plataforma de redes sociales X. “No lo olvidaremos, y tú tampoco deberías. Nadie está por encima de la ley”.</p><p>Bovino supuestamente le dijo a Breitbart que planea retirarse a fin de mes, y otros medios han reportado la noticia desde entonces. El Sun-Times no ha verificado de manera independiente esos informes. Un portavoz del DHS dijo, “El jefe Bovino no ha presentado ningún documento de jubilación”.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-d30000" name="module-d30000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Bovino se convirtió en <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2025/12/15/greg-bovino-es-la-estrella-del-show-de-deportacion-de-trump-investigamos-su-pasado" target="_blank" >la cara de los controversiales esfuerzos de aplicación de leyes de inmigración de la administración de Trump</a>, utilizando redes sociales para alardear sobre arrestos y enfrentarse a críticos, incluyendo a Pritzker. Los agentes bajo su mando en Chicago supuestamente realizaron <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2025/10/01/gregory-bovino-dice-que-los-arrestados-en-el-centro-de-chicago-fueron-elegidos-en-parte-por-como-se-ven" target="_blank" >detenciones basadas en la raza</a>, utilizaron tácticas arriesgadas y fuerza excesiva y, a menudo, desplegaron irritantes químicos contra los manifestantes.</p><p>La jueza del distrito Sara Ellis, ultimadamente restringió el uso de la fuerza por parte de los federales bajo la supervisión de Bovino en una orden que fue formalmente anulada a principios de este mes por una corte federal de apelaciones. Ellis acusó notablemente a Bovino de mentir bajo juramento sobre su justificación para el uso de gas lacrimógeno en el vecindario de La Villita.</p><p>Bovino fue <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/01/27/el-jefe-de-la-patrulla-fronteriza-gregory-bovino-y-algunos-agentes-salen-de-minneapolis" target="_blank" >enviado de regreso al sector de El Centro</a> en el sur de California tras el asesinato de Alex Pretti el 24 de enero en Minneapolis. Pretti, enfermero del Departamento de Asuntos de Veteranos, fue asesinado por dos agentes de la Oficina de Aduanas y Protección Fronteriza (CBP).</p><p>Después del tiroteo, Bovino dijo que Pretti “se acercó a la policía con un arma” y buscó “masacrar” a los agentes, una afirmación que fue rápidamente desmentida por evidencia en video, aunque Pretti llevaba legalmente un arma.</p><p>Bovino fue contratado antes del 6 de julio de 2008, lo que lo hace <a class="Link" href="https://www.cbp.gov/employee-resources/retirement/leo-cbpo/cbpo-retirement" target="_blank" >exento de la jubilación obligatoria de la agencia</a>, que es el último día del mes en el cual un empleado de CBP cumple 57 años y alcanza 20 años de servicio. Él cumplirá 56 años a finales de este mes.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Violet Miller

entries.title: White Sox' Mike Vasil to undergo season-ending Tommy John surgery
entries.summary: <p>GLENDALE, Ariz. — The White Sox bullpen took a major hit after the team announced that right-hander Mike Vasil would be undergoing season-ending Tommy John surgery.</p><p>“I think for me, and all these years of being able to pitch healthy and throwing a lot of innings, it’s just something that happens in today’s game,” Vasil said Tuesday. “Sometimes you can’t avoid it.”</p><p>He will have surgery performed by Dr. Keith Meister in Texas on April 2. Vasil said he woke up Sunday morning and realized the injury was more serious than he had imagined. He said the tests revealed that the injury was likely unavoidable due to wear and tear over time. Logging many injuries incurs a debt that eventually comes due.</p><p>“I’m going to do everything in my power to make it as speedy [recovery as possible] and also take the precautionary measures so that I’m back and ready,” Vasil said.</p><p>The injury is a blow to the bullpen and clubhouse. Vasil is a connector whose boisterous personality permeates throughout the locker room. Manager Will Venable said multiple players came up to him, imploring that Vasil remain with the team because of his positive personality.</p><p>"I had guys coming up to me [saying], ‘Gotta keep Vas around.,'" Venable said. "As soon as we heard that [surgery] was going to be the case with him, we all agreed that was the best thing for everybody."</p><p>On the mound, he was one of their more dependable arms, able to handle a multitude of roles and rack up innings — he was one of five Sox pitchers to throw at least 100 innings. Vasil also posted a 2.50 ERA last season and served as an opener, closer and multi-inning reliever for the club.</p><p>Without Vasil, the Sox will have to replace his ability to eat innings and serve in that multi-inning role. The injury can open the door for one of the Rule 5 draft selections — Jedixson Paez or Alexander Alberto — to make the team out of camp.</p><p>It's unlikely that Alberto gets that spot after a spotty spring in which he struggled with command and posted a 10.80 ERA. Paez, 22, doesn't have overwhelming stuff but succeeds by using multiple pitches and living in the strike zone. He hasn't pitched above the Single-A level, so being a multi-inning reliever could be beneficial as he acclimates to the majors.</p><p>Vasil's injury also opens the door for left-hander Chris Murphy, who entered camp with a shot at being in the rotation before settling into a bullpen role. He's stretched out and could provide bulk innings for the team. Left-hander Sean Newcomb started his career as a starter with the Braves, and was stretched out this spring, so he could also serve in that swingman role.</p><p>"Everyone has to step up," Venable said. "We’ll have Vas around. He’ll be around doing his rehab with us, which is great for us and is very important for our group and for him. [Vasil] was really kind of a Swiss Army knife for us and we’re gonna have to have guys step up in the bullpen."</p><p>The team is going to miss Vasil, but he’s also going to miss them. He said he’s never had a season where he couldn’t play baseball, so the isolation of rehab “is going to be difficult.” With his surgery already scheduled, Vasil has his eyes set on his return date.</p><p>“In my mind, April 2, 2027,” Vasil said of his target return date. “When I look at my career, I’m not by any means towards the end of it.”<br></p>
entries.author: Kyle Williams

entries.title: Watch: Results and analysis with WBEZ's Say More
Cubs' Seiya Suzuki will return to spring workouts after injuring knee in WBC
entries.summary: <p>MESA, Ariz. — The injury news on Seiya Suzuki could have been worse. The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" >Cubs</a> right fielder has a posterior cruciate ligament sprain in his right knee, manager Craig Counsell said, and while he may land on the injured list and miss Opening Day, he likely won't miss much time.</p><p>“We got good news,” Counsell said Tuesday at Cubs camp.</p><p>Suzuki has swelling in the knee but will participate in light activity, Counsell said, although he did not hit Tuesday. How he responds before Saturday, when a decision will likely be made, will determine whether he’ll be ready for Opening Day or go on the IL. Counsell described the injury as “minor in nature.”</p><p>In any case, it appears if Suzuki isn’t ready for Opening Day, he won’t be out for an extended time. If he’s on the Opening Day roster, he might initially see more time at designated hitter as the knee gets stronger.</p><p>“There’s nothing structurally that he can’t play,” Counsell said.</p><p>A long time on the IL would have been a significant loss for the Cubs, who are counting on Suzuki as an important middle-of-the-order run producer in a season with lofty team aspirations. Suzuki batted .245/.326/.478 with 32 homers in 151 games as the primary designated hitter for the Cubs last season and went 3-for-9 with two home runs, five walks, two runs scored and five RBI for Japan in the World Baseball Classic. He’s in the final year of a five-year, $85 million contract with the Cubs.</p><p>Talking to media for the first time since the injury, Suzuki said he was unsure about Opening Day, which was nine days away. It depends on how much the discomfort subsides, he said.</p><p>“I’m taking it day by day,” he said through a translator, “seeing where the pain level is at and once it dissipates, getting my body ready.”</p><p>Suzuki was in good spirits, joking that he would be doing sprints and squats Tuesday and saying he left his knee at second base Saturday night in Miami.</p><p>“Now we have to move forward and see,” Counsell said. “Is the first day of the season impacted or will it cost a week of the season? That’s what we’re trying to figure out as we gather more information.”</p><p>Suzuki said this is his first knee injury. He’s still limping slightly and has some swelling but “he’s not going to be shut down,” Counsell said. “We are going to continue activities.”</p><p>Suzuki felt “a lot of emotions” when he was hurt, including thoughts about his status for the opener at Wrigley Field.</p><p>“I promised myself I’m never going to slide head first,” he said.</p><p>Priority No. 1 for every team in spring training is staying healthy, but Suzuki joined <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/13/white-sox-to-be-mindful-of-the-long-term-with-kyle-teels-recovery-from-right-hamstring-strain" target="_blank" >White Sox catcher Kyle Teel</a> as World Baseball Classic casualties. Teel strained a hamstring legging out a double for Team Italy in the WBC and Suzuki injured his knee making a head first slide on an attempted steal of second base on Saturday, something he likely would not have attempted in a Cactus League game.</p><p>Suzuki’s focus shifted to the Cubs after Japan’s ouster Saturday. And his knee. He said the balance between wanting to help his team as soon as possible without rushing back too soon with the entire 162 games left on the schedule will be important.</p><p>“Communication with the team, wanting to play but you don’t want to rush it,” Suzuki said. “It’s important I take my time, make sure I am completely healthy and go from there.”</p><p></p><bsp-listcarousel class="PageListE" entries.title: Bring the taste of a faraway destination to your kitchen with lamb stew with figs and chickpeas
entries.summary: <p>Are you feeling a bout of wanderlust? Thanks to a long winter season, the urge to travel to a warm and far-flung destination is tantalizing. Yet, while we dream of an impulsive trip, the practicalities of daily life keep us rooted in place. So, why not improvise and bring the taste of a faraway destination home to your kitchen?</p><p>Turn to your spice cabinet and a cookbook for instant relief and cultural inspiration. It won’t be the same as a last-minute trip, of course, but at least you can stay at home in your pajamas.</p><p>This fragrant, meaty stew will transport you to Morocco. It’s subtly sweetened with figs, heady with harissa and redolent with ras el hanout — a North African spice blend consisting of a shop’s worth of dried spices.</p><p>Ras el hanout is an Arabic term that means “head of the shop,” signifying the best spices on offer. The blend varies from cook to cook, or merchant to merchant, and can contain upward of 50 spices. It’s a traditional spice used in tagines, couscous and stews, and typically includes cumin, coriander, cinnamon and ginger, among others. Purchasing a jar of prepared ras el hanout is an easy shortcut to build deep flavor in this stew. You can find it in the spice aisle of well-stocked supermarkets and specialty markets.</p><h3>Lamb Stew With Figs and Chickpeas</h3><p><b>Active time:</b> 40 minutes<br><b>Total time:</b> 3 1/2 to 4 hours<br><b>Yield:</b> Serves 5 to 6</p><p><b>Ingredients</b></p><p>2 1/2 to 3 pounds boneless lamb leg, excess fat trimmed, cut in 1 1/2- to 2-inch chunks<br>Kosher salt<br>Freshly ground black pepper<br>Olive oil<br>1 medium yellow onion, chopped<br>3 garlic cloves, chopped<br>1 tablespoon grated peeled fresh ginger<br>2 teaspoons ras el hanout<br>1 teaspoon ground cumin<br>1 teaspoon ground coriander<br>1/2 teaspoon ground paprika<br>1 (28-ounce) can Italian plum tomatoes<br>2 cups chicken stock<br>12 dried Turkish figs, halved<br>1 (2-inch) cinnamon stick<br>2 medium carrots, cut into 1/2-inch-thick slices<br>1 (15-ounce) can chickpeas, drained<br>1 tablespoon honey or light brown sugar<br>1 to 2 tablespoons harissa or red chile paste<br>1/4 cup chopped fresh cilantro leaves and tender stems, plus more for garnish</p><p><b>Directions</b></p><p>Heat the oven to 300 degrees. Generously season the lamb with salt and pepper.</p><p>Heat 1 tablespoon oil in a large Dutch oven or ovenproof pot with lid over medium-high heat. Add the lamb in batches, without overcrowding the pan. Brown the meat well on all sides, then transfer the lamb to a bowl. Repeat with the remaining lamb.</p><p>Pour off all but 1 tablespoon fat from the pot (or add 1 tablespoon oil, if dry). Add the onion and saute until it is soft without coloring, 2 to 3 minutes, stirring up any brown bits. Add the garlic and ginger and saute until fragrant, about 30 seconds. Add the ras el hanout, cumin, coriander and paprika and stir for 15 to 30 seconds to lightly toast the spices.</p><p>Add the tomatoes and their juice, crushing each tomato with your hands as you add them to the pot. Add the lamb (and any collected juices from the bowl), the stock, figs, cinnamon stick, 1 teaspoon salt and 1/2 teaspoon black pepper. The lamb should be completely submerged in the stock. If not, add additional stock to cover.</p><p>Bring the stew to a simmer, cover the pot and transfer to the oven. Cook until the lamb is very tender, 2 1/2 to 3 hours, stirring occasionally.</p><p>While the lamb is cooking, heat 1 tablespoon oil in a skillet. Add the carrots and cook until crisp-tender, 6 to 8 minutes.</p><p>Transfer the stew to the stovetop. Add the carrots, chickpeas, brown sugar and harissa. Simmer, uncovered, over medium-low heat for 10 to 12 minutes, then taste for seasoning. Stir in the cilantro.</p><p>Ladle the stew into bowls and serve with couscous, if desired. Garnish with additional cilantro.</p><p><i>Lynda Balslev is an award-winning writer, cookbook author and recipe developer, and authors the blog TasteFood.</i></p>
entries.author: Lynda Balslev | TasteFood

entries.title: Marcus Samuelsson shares a comfort-food recipe for oxtail pepperpot with dumplings
entries.summary: <p>Oxtail is one of my favorite meats, and I like it best when it has been slow-cooked for hours, so I recommend cooking it the day before and letting it sit overnight. What makes this dish from my cookbook “The Rise” so homey and delicious is the mix of the oxtail and the dumplings, which everyone can relate to as being an example of comfort food at its finest.</p><p>This traditional Caribbean dish — mostly from Guyana — is made by stewing meat in a dark, rich gravy flavored with cinnamon, brown sugar, hot chiles and cassareep, a special brown sauce made from cassava root. African Americans adapted the recipe using oxtail instead of offal, which are the internal organs of butchered animals. Regardless, this is a dish that only gets better with time in the pot.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-d40000" name="image-d40000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fe53c80/2147483647/strip/true/crop/819x460+0+282/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0c%2Fbb%2F924560b848c2bfbb313364b1f385%2Fap26036672192812.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c982b2f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/819x460+0+282/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0c%2Fbb%2F924560b848c2bfbb313364b1f385%2Fap26036672192812.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fe9ca8f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/819x460+0+282/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0c%2Fbb%2F924560b848c2bfbb313364b1f385%2Fap26036672192812.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="This image released by Voracious shows a recipe for oxtail pepperpot with dumplings, from the cookbook "The Rise" by Marcus Samuelsson. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fe9ca8f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/819x460+0+282/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0c%2Fbb%2F924560b848c2bfbb313364b1f385%2Fap26036672192812.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e1653aa/2147483647/strip/true/crop/819x460+0+282/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0c%2Fbb%2F924560b848c2bfbb313364b1f385%2Fap26036672192812.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fe9ca8f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/819x460+0+282/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0c%2Fbb%2F924560b848c2bfbb313364b1f385%2Fap26036672192812.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Oxtail pepperpot with dumplings is one of the recipes from the cookbook “The Rise” by Marcus Samuelsson.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Angie Mosier/Voracious via AP</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>Oxtail Pepperpot with Dumplings</h3><p><b>Servings:</b> 4 to 6</p><p><b>Ingredients</b><br><b>For the oxtail</b><br>1 (4-pound) piece oxtail<br>1 ½ teaspoons kosher salt<br>1 teaspoon freshly ground black pepper<br>½ cup vegetable oil<br>2 carrots, peeled and diced<br>1 onion, diced<br>21 cloves garlic, minced<br>7 tablespoons minced ginger (3-inch piece)<br>2 plum tomatoes, diced<br>2 scallions, sliced<br>1 Scotch bonnet (or habanero) chile, stemmed and chopped<br>3 sprigs fresh thyme<br>7 tablespoons brown sugar<br>2 tablespoons soy sauce<br>2 tablespoons ketchup<br>1 tablespoon whole allspice berries<br>6 cups chicken stock</p><p><b>For the dumplings</b><br>2 cups all-purpose flour<br>2½ tablespoons cornmeal<br>1 teaspoon kosher salt<br>½ cup plus 2 tablespoons water</p><p><b>Directions</b></p><p>For the oxtail: Season the oxtail on all sides with salt and pepper. Heat ¼ cup of the oil in a large (8-quart) Dutch oven set over medium-high heat. When the oil shimmers, add the oxtail and brown on both sides, about 15 minutes.</p><p>Remove the oxtail to a paper-towel-lined dish. Heat the remaining ¼ cup oil in the Dutch oven and add the carrots, onion, garlic, ginger, tomatoes, scallions, chile, thyme, brown sugar, soy sauce, ketchup and allspice and stir to combine. Return the oxtail to the pot, add the chicken stock, and bring to a simmer. Reduce the heat to maintain a simmer and cook, covered, for 2½ hours, or until the oxtail is tender and the meat is falling away from the bone.</p><p>For the dumplings: Place the flour, cornmeal and salt in a medium bowl and stir to combine. Add the water and use your hands to work the mixture into a dough ball. Knead the dough in the bowl for 2 to 3 minutes. Divide the dough in half and cover one half with a damp towel.</p><p>Continue to knead one dough ball for 5 minutes, or until smooth. Roll the piece of dough into a 21- to 24-inch snakelike piece. Cut the dough into 1-inch pieces, set on a baking sheet, and cover with a damp towel.</p><p>Repeat with remaining dough ball.</p><p>Stir the dumplings into the oxtail stew for the last 30 minutes of cooking time and cook until dumplings are tender and cooked through.</p><p>Serve the stew with rice and peas.<br>___</p><p>Marcus Samuelsson is the chef behind many restaurants worldwide. He is also co-chair of the Careers through Culinary Arts Program (C-CAP), which focuses on underserved youth, and coproduces the annual Harlem EatUp! festival, which celebrates the food, art and culture of Harlem.</p><p>Excerpted from “The Rise” by Marcus Samuelsson. Copyright (copyright) 2020 by Marcus Samuelsson. Used with permission of Voracious, an imprint of Little, Brown and Company. New York, NY. All rights reserved.</p>

entries.title: White Sox' Munetaka Murakami homers in return to spring training
entries.summary: <p>GLENDALE, Ariz. — White Sox first baseman <b>Munetaka Murakami </b>wasted little time displaying the prodigious power that has fans salivating.</p><p>On a 1-2 count, Murakami connected on a 96.6 mph fastball in the zone for a solo shot to center field for his first homer of spring training. Murakami returned to the Sox on Monday and played in his first game on Tuesday since returning from the World Baseball Classic. Murakami's last day with the Sox was on Feb. 26.</p><p>"Compared to when I left Arizona, I feel much better as far as conditioning, as well," Murakami said through interpreter <b>Kenzo Yagi</b>. "I’m seeing the ball better, I’m swinging better, so I’ll keep that approach up and try to hit as much as possible.”</p><p>Murakami was grateful for his WBC experience with Team Japan, calling it a "pleasure" to play alongside "incredible athletes and teammates." That time away from the Sox, however, put him behind schedule in terms of building camaraderie with his teammates and learning the team's systems.</p><p>"[Third-base coach] <b>Justin Jirschele</b> and the group did a really nice job up front getting him going on that, and now [it’s] kind of picking up where he left off," manager Will Venable said. "We have put a lot of different stuff in since he was gone, so these last couple of days have been some classroom sessions, getting up to speed on some stuff."</p><p>After playing primarily third base in Japan, Murakami is ready to man first base.</p><p>“I’m getting comfortable every single day," he said. "I think I’m ready.”</p><h3><b>Sox’ rotation set</b></h3><p>White Sox right-hander <b>Jonathan Cannon</b> was optioned to Triple-A Charlotte on Tuesday.</p><p>"Some of the things we asked him to do with count leverage, getting ahead of guys — the race to [two strikes] — [Cannon] did a really good job of controlling the running game, burying holds and did a really nice job of doing that," manager Will Venable said. "It just happens that we had five guys ahead of him. He’s No. 6 guy, and for us to maintain that depth, it was important for him to be optioned and continue to be stretched out."</p><p>Following Opening Day starter<b> Shane Smith</b> in the rotation will be right-hander <b>Sean Burke</b>, left-hander <b>Anthony Kay</b>, and right-handers <b>Davis Martin</b> and <b>Erick Fedde</b>.</p><p>Cannon posted a 4.09 ERA over four spring appearances (one start). In his last outing, he allowed two runs on five hits with four walks.</p><p>Venable said the team wants Cannon to continue getting ahead of guys, throwing strikes in the zone, and controlling it. Pitching depth will be important for the Sox, as evidenced by <b>Mike Vasil</b> having season-ending Tommy John surgery, so it's likely that Cannon will start on the South Side this season.</p><h3><b>White Sox 6, A’s 4</b></h3><p>• Left fielder <b>Andrew Benintendi </b>hit his first homer of spring training in the third inning.</p><p>• Left-hander <b>Anthony Kay </b>threw 4 2/3 innings, allowing one run on four hits with five strikeouts. He has one more start this spring.</p><p>• Right-hander <b>Jedixson Paez </b>threw 2 1/3 innings, allowing one run and one hit with three strikeouts.</p><p>• Second baseman<b> Chase Meidroth</b> was 2-for-4 with a homer.</p><p><b>On deck: </b>Off day on Wednesday.<br></p>
entries.author: Kyle Williams
